tier_1_regimens.site: Oral cavity, Oropharynx, Hypopharynx, Glottic larynx, Supraglottic larynx
tier_1_regimens.setting: Definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy for locally advanced disease

trial_data.outcomes_summary: Concurrent high-dose cisplatin with RT improves overall survival compared to RT alone. Accelerated fractionation with two cycles of concurrent cisplatin versus standard fractionation with three cycles of concurrent cisplatin showed no significant difference in OS.

tier_1_regimens.notes: The preferred chemoradiotherapy approach for fit patients with locally advanced disease remains concurrent cisplatin and radiotherapy. High-dose cisplatin (100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks) is the most studied effective regimen. When carboplatin and fluorouracil are used, the recommended regimen is standard fractionation plus 3 cycles of chemotherapy. Other fraction sizes, multiagent chemotherapy, other dosing schedules of cisplatin, or altered fractionation with chemotherapy are efficacious, and there is no consensus on the optimal approach. In general, the use of concurrent systemic therapy/RT carries a high toxicity burden; multiagent chemotherapy will likely further increase the toxicity burden. Systemic therapy/RT should be performed by an experienced team and should include substantial supportive care. [27, 162, 288]

tier_1_regimens.site: Oral cavity, Oropharynx, Hypopharynx, Glottic larynx, Supraglottic larynx
tier_1_regimens.setting: Definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy for locally advanced disease

trial_data.outcomes_summary: Concurrent carboplatin/fluorouracil with RT is effective. Altered fractionation did not improve outcomes when compared with conventional fractionation given with concurrent chemotherapy.

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is a preferred option for concurrent chemoradiotherapy. Based on published data, when carboplatin and fluorouracil are used, the recommended regimen is standard fractionation plus 3 cycles of chemotherapy. Other fraction sizes, multiagent chemotherapy, other dosing schedules of cisplatin, or altered fractionation with chemotherapy are efficacious, and there is no consensus on the optimal approach. Concurrent chemoradiation increases acute toxicity compared to radiation alone. [191]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Weekly Cisplatin (40 mg/m2) with Concurrent RT
tier_1_regimens.site: Oral cavity, Oropharynx, Hypopharynx, Glottic larynx, Supraglottic larynx
tier_1_regimens.setting: Definitive or postoperative concurrent chemoradiotherapy

drugs.name: Radiation therapy
drugs.dose: 70 Gy (2.0 Gy/fraction) for definitive; 60-66 Gy (2.0 Gy/fraction) for postoperative

trial_data.outcomes_summary: Weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 was non-inferior to high-dose every-3-week cisplatin with respect to OS in patients with high-risk resected locally advanced SCCHN. High-grade neutropenia, infections, renal and hearing impairment were less common with weekly cisplatin regimen, whereas thrombocytopenia was more common. [305]

tier_1_regimens.notes: If the clinician has a patient-specific concern about the toxicity of high-dose cisplatin, a weekly cisplatin regimen at a dose of 40 mg/m2/week may be substituted. Weekly doses of cisplatin of <40 mg/m2 should not be utilized in this setting because survival outcomes were inferior. [7,8,9,10,11]

tier_1_regimens.site: Oral cavity, Oropharynx, Hypopharynx, Larynx (glottic and supraglottic)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Induction/sequential therapy for locally advanced disease

trial_data.outcomes_summary: TPF induction chemotherapy, compared to cisplatin plus fluorouracil, was associated with reduced risk of death (HR 0.72; 95% CI 0.63-0.83; P<.001) and greater reductions in progression, locoregional failure, and distant failure. [20,21,22,23,334]

tier_1_regimens.notes: When used as induction chemotherapy for SCCHN, docetaxel with cisplatin/infusional fluorouracil is a category 1 preferred recommendation. The role of induction chemotherapy in the management of locally advanced SCCHN has generated considerable discussion and debate within the NCCN Panel. Induction chemotherapy should only be administered at sites with expertise in these regimens because of challenges associated with appropriate patient selection and management of treatment-related toxicities. Residual toxicity from induction chemotherapy may complicate subsequent delivery of definitive RT or systemic therapy/RT. [20,21,22,23,334]

drugs.name: Cisplatin
drugs.dose: Not specified (only if extranodal extension and/or positive margin)

trial_data.outcomes_summary: In patients with PD-L1 CPS ≥10, EFS at 36 months was 59.8% with pembrolizumab vs 45.9% with control (HR 0.66; 95% CI 0.49–0.88; P=.004). In those with CPS ≥1, EFS at 36 months was 58.2% vs 44.9% (HR 0.70; 95% CI 0.55–0.89; P=.003). Pathological complete response occurred in 4.2%, 3.1%, and 3.0% in CPS ≥10, CPS ≥1, and total populations respectively. [352]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Perioperative pembrolizumab (neoadjuvant and adjuvant) added to standard of care (surgery and adjuvant radiotherapy with or without concomitant cisplatin) showed improvement in event-free survival. The FDA approved neoadjuvant pembrolizumab followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab/RT followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab for patients with resectable, PD-L1–positive (CPS ≥1), locally advanced SCCHN. For patients with ENE and/or positive margins, cisplatin should be added to adjuvant pembrolizumab/RT. [25,26,352]

trial_data.outcomes_summary: Patients with ENE and/or positive resection margins benefited from the addition of cisplatin to postoperative RT. For those with multiple involved regional nodes without ENE, there was no survival advantage. [141,142]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Postoperative concurrent chemoradiation is recommended for patients with high-risk pathologic features: extranodal extension and/or positive surgical margins. The preferred systemic agent is cisplatin. Weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2 is non-inferior to high-dose every-3-week cisplatin based on JCOG1008. [33-40]

trial_data.outcomes_summary: In cisplatin-ineligible patients, docetaxel with RT improved 2-year DFS (42.0% vs 30.3%; HR 0.67; 95% CI 0.52–0.87; P=.002) and median OS (25.5 vs 15.3 months; P=.035) compared to RT alone. [14,145]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Study results support use of docetaxel as a radiosensitizer for patients undergoing adjuvant treatment who are cisplatin ineligible. [14]

trial_data.outcomes_summary: Patients randomized to receive docetaxel experienced a 31% reduction in DFS failure rate (HR 0.69; 95% CI 0.50–0.96; P=.01) and a 44% reduction in mortality (HR 0.56; 95% CI 0.39–0.82; P=.001) compared to cetuximab/cisplatin arm. [146]

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with high-risk adverse pathologic features following surgery (extranodal extension and/or positive margins) who are ineligible for platinum therapy, docetaxel/cetuximab is a category 2B option. [41]

tier_1_regimens.notes: For select histologies. [30]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant therapy after resection

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.dose: Not specified (IV) or nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous

tier_1_regimens.notes: While adjuvant systemic therapy may be used for mucosal melanoma, data to support its use are far fewer than for cutaneous melanoma. Options may include nivolumab (category 2B) or cisplatin/temozolomide (category 2B). Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab. [k]

tier_1_regimens.setting: Adjuvant therapy after resection

trial_data.outcomes_summary: Adjuvant temozolomide plus cisplatin significantly improved relapse-free survival (15.5 vs 9.9 months; HR 0.622; P=.001) and distant metastasis-free survival (19.5 vs 12.7 months; HR 0.705; P=.025) compared to high-dose interferon alpha-2b. [838]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 2B option for adjuvant therapy in resected mucosal melanoma. [k]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Cisplatin + Reirradiation with Concurrent Systemic Therapy (for recurrent/persistent disease with prior RT)
tier_1_regimens.site: Oral cavity, Oropharynx, Hypopharynx, Larynx, Nasopharynx
tier_1_regimens.setting: Reirradiation with concurrent systemic therapy for locoregional recurrence with prior RT

drugs.name: Radiation therapy (reirradiation)
drugs.dose: Conventional fractionation: 56-60 Gy (postoperative) or 66-70 Gy (definitive); Accelerated: 60-70 Gy at 1.2-1.5 Gy/fraction twice daily; Hyperfractionation for NPC: 64.8 Gy in 54 fractions twice daily

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Preferred (Reirradiation + Concurrent Systemic Therapy for non-nasopharyngeal)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Reirradiation strategies can be considered for patients who develop locoregional recurrent or persistent disease or second primaries at ≥6 months after initial radiotherapy, can receive additional doses of at least 60 Gy, and can tolerate concurrent chemotherapy. When concurrent systemic therapy is given with conventionally fractionated reirradiation, cisplatin with concurrent RT is the preferred option. [7,33]

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Weekly Carboplatin + Concurrent RT (following induction therapy or for recurrent/persistent disease)
tier_1_regimens.setting: Systemic therapy/RT following induction therapy, or combination chemotherapy for recurrent/persistent disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Weekly carboplatin is a reasonable agent to use with RT in patients previously treated with induction chemotherapy. [31,32]

tier_2_handbook.definition: Head and neck cancers encompass a diverse group of malignancies arising from the mucosal surfaces of the upper aerodigestive tract. The oral cavity (including mucosal lip) comprises the buccal mucosa, floor of mouth, oral tongue, alveolar ridge, retromolar trigone, and hard palate [OR-1]. The oropharynx includes the base of tongue, tonsils, soft palate, and posterior pharyngeal wall [ORPH-1]. The hypopharynx extends from the hyoid bone to the cricoid cartilage and is divided into pyriform sinus, posterior pharyngeal wall, and postcricoid area [HYPO-1]. The nasopharynx is the uppermost part of the pharynx, posterior to the nasal cavity, and is strongly associated with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection [NASO-1]. The larynx is subdivided into supraglottis, glottis, and subglottis [GLOT-1, SUPRA-1]. Paranasal sinus tumors arise from the ethmoid and maxillary sinuses [ETHM-1, MAXI-1]. Salivary gland tumors originate from major (parotid, submandibular, sublingual) or minor salivary glands distributed throughout the aerodigestive tract [SALI-1]. Mucosal melanoma of the head and neck occurs in the sinonasal tract, oral cavity, pharynx, and larynx [MM-1]. Occult primary cancer is defined as metastatic carcinoma in a cervical lymph node without an identifiable primary site after thorough investigation [OCC-1]. Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) accounts for >90% of these tumors [Discussion, MS-1]. Embryologically, these structures derive from the pharyngeal arches, pouches, and foregut endoderm, with distinct anatomical boundaries defined by the AJCC staging system [ST-1 to ST-19].
tier_2_handbook.subtypes: The predominant histologic subtype is squamous cell carcinoma, representing >90% of head and neck cancers [Discussion, MS-1]. Within SCC, oropharyngeal tumors are further classified by HPV status using p16 immunohistochemistry as a surrogate; p16-positive (HPV-associated) and p16-negative (HPV-unrelated) diseases are recognized as distinct entities with separate staging and prognosis [ORPH-1, ORPH-B]. Other histologic subtypes include adenocarcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma, acinic cell carcinoma, and malignant myoepithelial tumors arising from salivary glands [SALI-1]. Minor salivary gland tumors show similar diversity. For paranasal sinus tumors, subtypes include squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma (including intestinal-type), esthesioneuroblastoma (olfactory neuroblastoma), undifferentiated carcinoma (sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma [SNUC], sinonasal neuroendocrine carcinoma [SNEC]), and NUT midline carcinoma [ETHM-1, MAXI-1]. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is classified by the World Health Organization into keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, nonkeratinizing carcinoma (most common, EBV-associated), and undifferentiated carcinoma [NASO-1]. Laryngeal cancers are predominantly SCC but can include rare variants such as verrucous carcinoma, basaloid SCC, and sarcomatoid carcinoma [GLOT-1]. Mucosal melanoma is a distinct entity with melanocytic differentiation, confirmed by immunohistochemistry (HMB-45, S100, Melan-A) [MM-1]. Lymphoma and sarcoma may also present in head and neck sites and are managed according to dedicated guidelines [ETHM-1, MAXI-1]. The frequency of subtypes varies: SCC is most common overall; salivary gland tumors are rare; mucosal melanoma is exceedingly rare but aggressive. Key associations include HPV with oropharyngeal SCC, EBV with nasopharyngeal carcinoma, and tobacco/alcohol with HPV-negative SCC of the oral cavity, larynx, and hypopharynx.
tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC) develops through two major molecular pathways: HPV-associated and HPV-unrelated. HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancers are driven by expression of the E6 and E7 oncogenes, which inactivate tumor suppressor proteins p53 and pRb, respectively, leading to genomic instability. Upregulation of p16 protein, a surrogate marker of HPV DNA, results from pRb inactivation. Genetic profiling shows HPV-positive tumors are molecularly distinct from HPV-negative HNSCC, with fewer TP53 mutations but frequent alterations in PIK3CA (activating mutations) and loss of TRAF3 [Discussion MS-3]. HPV-negative HNSCC is characterized by mutations in TP53 (85-90% of tumors), CDKN2A (p16) inactivation, and amplification of CCND1 and EGFR. EGFR overexpression is common and associated with poor prognosis [Discussion MS-21]. Promoter mutations in TERT are frequent. Whole-exome sequencing has identified recurrent alterations in NOTCH1, FAT1, and CASP8. For salivary gland tumors, translocations are hallmark events: mucoepidermoid carcinoma has CRTC1-MAML2 fusion; adenoid cystic carcinoma shows MYB-NFIB fusion; secretory carcinoma (mammary analogue) has ETV6-NTRK3 fusion. Salivary duct carcinoma frequently shows HER2 amplification and androgen receptor expression [SALI-B]. Sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma (SNUC) may harbor IDH2 R172X mutations. NUT midline carcinoma is defined by NUTM1 rearrangements. Mucosal melanoma shows distinct genomic features: frequent mutations in NRAS (15-19%), KIT (7-26%), and NF1 (18%), with lower BRAF mutation rates (5-8%) compared to cutaneous melanoma [Discussion MS-65]. Recurrent amplifications of CDK4, MDM2, and TERT are common. In nasopharyngeal carcinoma, EBV infection is a key etiologic factor, with latent membrane protein 1 (LMP1) driving NF-κB signaling and genomic instability.
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: In 2026, an estimated 72,770 new cases of oral cavity, pharyngeal, and laryngeal cancers and 17,110 deaths are projected in the United States, accounting for approximately 3.4% of new cancer cases [Discussion MS-2; Siegel et al. 2026, reference 1]. The incidence of HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer is increasing, particularly in men, while HPV-negative (tobacco/alcohol-related) cancer is declining [Discussion MS-3; Chaturvedi et al. J Clin Oncol 2011;29:4294-4301, reference 14]. The attributable fraction of HPV in oropharyngeal cancer is approximately 90% in the United States and parts of Europe [Discussion MS-3]. Patients with HPV-positive cancer tend to be younger (median age at diagnosis 60-64 years) and have better prognosis, while HPV-negative patients are older (median age 66 years) and have higher risk of second primaries [Discussion MS-3]. Oral cavity cancer has a higher incidence in males, with strong racial disparities: Black men have higher rates of oral cavity and pharyngeal cancers compared to White men. Laryngeal cancer incidence is 4-5 times higher in males than females, with highest rates in Black males [ST-8]. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is rare in the United States but endemic in Southeast Asia, southern China, North Africa, and the Arctic, with incidence rates 20-50 per 100,000 in high-risk regions; rates in the US are <1 per 100,000 [Discussion MS-41]. The male-to-female ratio is 2-3:1 for nasopharyngeal carcinoma. For paranasal sinus cancers, the age-adjusted incidence is very low (<1 per 100,000); ethmoid sinus tumors are rarer than maxillary sinus tumors. Mucosal melanoma accounts for <1% of all melanoma cases but has a poor prognosis; the incidence of sinonasal mucosal melanoma is increasing [Discussion MS-65]. Salivary gland tumors have an annual incidence of about 3 per 100,000; 20% of parotid tumors are malignant, rising to 50% for submandibular and 80% for minor salivary gland tumors [Discussion MS-62]. Occult primary head and neck cancer accounts for approximately 5% of patients presenting to referral centers [Discussion MS-60].

risk_factors.factor: Tobacco use (cigarettes, cigars, pipes, smokeless tobacco)
risk_factors.detail: Strongly associated with oral cavity, oropharynx (HPV-negative), hypopharynx, larynx, and nasopharynx cancers. Risk increases with duration and intensity (pack-years). Smoking decreases efficacy of radiotherapy and adversely affects survival [Discussion MS-5, MS-21].
risk_factors.strength: Established causal relationship; RR 5-25 for current smokers vs. never-smokers

risk_factors.detail: Synergistic with tobacco; particularly for oral cavity, oropharynx, hypopharynx, and larynx cancers. Heavy drinking (≥3 drinks/day) significantly increases risk [Discussion MS-5].
risk_factors.strength: Established causal; RR 2-5 for heavy drinkers vs. nondrinkers, synergistic with tobacco

risk_factors.detail: Primary cause of oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (tonsil and base of tongue). HPV16 accounts for ~90% of HPV-positive cases. Oral HPV infection increases risk of oropharyngeal cancer [Discussion MS-3].
risk_factors.strength: Causal; attributable fraction ~90% in US oropharyngeal cancers; OR ~15-50 for HPV16 seropositivity

risk_factors.detail: Key etiologic factor for nasopharyngeal carcinoma, particularly in endemic regions. EBV DNA detectable in tumor tissue and plasma; high viral load associated with worse prognosis [Discussion MS-41].
risk_factors.strength: Causal; EBV positivity in >95% of nonkeratinizing nasopharyngeal carcinoma

risk_factors.factor: Occupational exposures (wood dust, leather, nickel, formaldehyde)
risk_factors.detail: Specifically for sinonasal cancers. Wood dust exposure is strongly linked to adenocarcinoma of the ethmoid sinus. Nickel refining, leather dust, and formaldehyde are also associated [Discussion MS-52].
risk_factors.strength: Established; wood dust RR ~5-50 for ethmoid adenocarcinoma

risk_factors.detail: Family history of head and neck cancer increases risk. Patients with Fanconi anemia, dyskeratosis congenita, and Li-Fraumeni syndrome have elevated risk. Genome-wide association studies have identified risk loci at 4q21, 4q25, 12q24, and other regions [OR-1].
risk_factors.strength: Moderate to high in specific syndromes; modest for common variants

risk_factors.detail: Incidence rates 2-4 times higher in men across most subsites. For oropharyngeal cancer, the male-to-female ratio is 4-5:1 for HPV-positive cases [Discussion MS-3].
risk_factors.strength: Established; male sex is an independent risk factor

risk_factors.detail: Increased risk of HPV-related head and neck cancers. HIV-positive individuals have higher rates of oral and oropharyngeal cancers [Discussion MS-4].

risk_factors.factor: Betel quid and areca nut chewing
risk_factors.detail: Common in South and Southeast Asia; associated with oral cavity and oropharyngeal cancers. Synergistic with tobacco and alcohol [Discussion MS-5].
risk_factors.strength: Causal; RR 5-20 for betel quid chewers

risk_factors.factor: Poor oral hygiene and chronic irritation
risk_factors.detail: Chronic dental trauma, poor dental status, and chronic oral infections may contribute to oral cavity cancer risk [Discussion MS-31].

protective_factors: HPV vaccination: vaccination against HPV types 6, 11, 16, 18 (and nonavalent vaccine covering additional high-risk types) reduces oral HPV infections. Data from NHANES 2011-2014 show reduced vaccine-type oral HPV prevalence in vaccinated individuals (0.1% vs. 1.6%, P = .008) [Discussion MS-4; Chaturvedi et al. 2018]. The FDA expanded the HPV vaccine indication to include prevention of oropharyngeal cancer [Discussion MS-4].

Smoking cessation: quitting smoking reduces the risk of head and neck cancer and improves treatment outcomes. The risk of developing cancer declines after cessation, approaching that of never-smokers after 10-20 years [Discussion MS-5]. All patients should be advised to quit and remain abstinent, especially during radiotherapy [TEAM-1].

Alcohol moderation: reducing alcohol intake lowers risk of oral cavity, pharyngeal, and laryngeal cancers. Combining smoking cessation with alcohol reduction provides synergistic benefit [Discussion MS-5].

Diet and nutrition: high intake of fruits and vegetables, particularly those rich in carotenoids and vitamin C, may reduce risk of oral and pharyngeal cancers. The evidence is supportive but not definitive [Discussion MS-5].
Oral and dental care: regular dental care and maintaining oral hygiene may reduce risk of oral cavity cancer by limiting chronic inflammation and infection [DENT-A].

Occupational exposure controls: reducing exposure to wood dust, leather dust, and formaldehyde through ventilation, protective equipment, and industrial hygiene lowers risk of sinonasal cancers [Discussion MS-52].

EBV screening in endemic regions: plasma EBV DNA testing can detect early-stage nasopharyngeal carcinoma, allowing for earlier diagnosis and improved outcomes. In high-risk populations, screening has been shown to increase the proportion of early-stage diagnoses [NASO-1; Discussion MS-41].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Head and neck cancers encompass diverse subsites with distinct presentations. Oral cavity cancers (buccal mucosa, floor of mouth, oral tongue, alveolar ridge, retromolar trigone, hard palate) typically present as a non-healing ulcer or mass, with pain, dysphagia, or odynophagia [OR-1]. Oropharyngeal cancers (base of tongue, tonsil, soft palate, posterior pharyngeal wall) often present with a neck mass, especially in HPV-positive disease, along with sore throat, dysphagia, or otalgia [ORPH-1]. HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer is increasing in incidence, especially in men, and patients tend to be younger with less tobacco exposure [Discussion MS-3]. HPV-negative oropharyngeal cancer is linked to tobacco and alcohol, presenting with larger primaries and worse prognosis. Laryngeal cancers: glottic tumors cause early hoarseness, while supraglottic tumors present with dysphagia, otalgia, or neck mass; subglottic tumors are rare and present with stridor or dyspnea [GLOT-1, SUPRA-1]. Hypopharyngeal cancers (pyriform sinus, postcricoid, posterior pharyngeal wall) often present late with dysphagia, odynophagia, referred otalgia, and neck mass; prognosis is poor [HYPO-1]. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) is endemic in Southeast Asia and associated with EBV; classic presentation includes cervical lymphadenopathy, epistaxis, nasal obstruction, conductive hearing loss, and cranial neuropathies [NASO-1]. Sinonasal tumors (ethmoid and maxillary sinus) present with unilateral nasal obstruction, epistaxis, facial pain, or proptosis; histologies include squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, esthesioneuroblastoma, and undifferentiated carcinoma [ETHM-1, MAXI-1]. Salivary gland tumors (parotid, submandibular, minor) typically present as a painless, slow-growing mass; malignancy is suspected with facial nerve palsy, rapid growth, or fixation [SALI-1]. Occult primary presents as a cervical lymph node metastasis without an identifiable primary after thorough workup [OCC-1]. Mucosal melanoma of the head and neck presents as a pigmented or non-pigmented lesion in the nasal cavity, sinuses, or oral cavity, often with epistaxis or mass [MM-1].

symptoms.name: Neck mass
symptoms.detail: Painless or painful cervical lymphadenopathy, often firm or fixed, may be cystic in HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer.
symptoms.frequency: Very common in oropharyngeal, hypopharyngeal, and nasopharyngeal cancers; may be first sign in occult primary.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent neck mass >2 weeks requires urgent investigation, especially if >3 cm, fixed, or matted.

symptoms.name: Hoarseness
symptoms.detail: Change in voice quality lasting >3 weeks; common in glottic laryngeal cancer.
symptoms.frequency: Present in >90% of glottic cancers, often early stage.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent hoarseness >3 weeks warrants laryngeal examination.

symptoms.name: Dysphagia/Odynophagia
symptoms.detail: Pain or difficulty swallowing, often with referred otalgia.
symptoms.frequency: Common in oropharyngeal, hypopharyngeal, and supraglottic laryngeal cancers.
symptoms.alarm: Progressive dysphagia or odynophagia, especially with weight loss.

symptoms.name: Oral ulcer or mass
symptoms.detail: Non-healing ulcer, erythroplakia, or leukoplakia in the oral cavity.
symptoms.alarm: Lesion >2 weeks without healing, especially in high-risk patients (tobacco/alcohol).

symptoms.name: Epistaxis and nasal obstruction
symptoms.detail: Unilateral epistaxis, nasal congestion, or discharge.
symptoms.frequency: Common in sinonasal and nasopharyngeal cancers.
symptoms.alarm: Unilateral symptoms, especially with associated cranial neuropathy or proptosis.

symptoms.name: Otalgia
symptoms.detail: Referred ear pain, often unilateral, without otoscopic findings.
symptoms.frequency: Common in oropharyngeal, hypopharyngeal, and laryngeal cancers.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent unilateral otalgia, especially with dysphagia or neck mass.

symptoms.name: Cranial neuropathy
symptoms.detail: Facial numbness, diplopia, or other cranial nerve deficits.
symptoms.frequency: More common in nasopharyngeal and sinonasal cancers due to skull base invasion.
symptoms.alarm: Any cranial nerve deficit, particularly V1–V3 or VI, warrants urgent imaging.

signs.name: Visible or palpable primary lesion
signs.detail: Ulcerated, exophytic, or infiltrative mass in oral cavity, oropharynx, or larynx on examination.
signs.frequency: Present in most oral cavity and laryngeal cancers; oropharyngeal tumors may be occult.

signs.name: Cervical lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Enlarged, firm, or matted lymph nodes in the neck; may be cystic in HPV-positive disease.
signs.frequency: Present in 30–60% of patients at diagnosis depending on subsite.
signs.alarm: Multiple, bilateral, or fixed nodes suggest advanced disease.

signs.name: Vocal cord immobility
signs.detail: Impaired or fixed vocal cord on laryngoscopy, indicating paraglottic space invasion.
signs.alarm: Indicates advanced laryngeal or thyroid cancer.

signs.name: Trismus
signs.detail: Limited mouth opening due to pterygoid muscle invasion.
signs.frequency: Seen in advanced oropharyngeal and maxillary sinus cancers.

signs.name: Facial nerve palsy
signs.detail: Weakness of facial muscles, usually in parotid malignancies.
signs.frequency: Present in 10–20% of malignant parotid tumors.
signs.alarm: Pathognomonic for malignancy; requires urgent evaluation.

signs.name: Proptosis or periorbital swelling
signs.detail: Forward displacement of the eye or eyelid edema due to orbital invasion.
signs.frequency: Seen in sinonasal cancers extending to orbit.

atypical_or_missed: HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer often presents as a cystic neck mass in a young non-smoker, mimicking branchial cleft cyst; delay in diagnosis is common if FNA shows reactive cells or if HPV testing is not performed [ORPH-1].
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma may present solely with serous otitis media (conductive hearing loss) due to eustachian tube obstruction; this can be misattributed to Eustachian tube dysfunction in adults.
Supraglottic laryngeal cancer may present with subtle dysphagia or throat discomfort, and hoarseness occurs late, leading to advanced stage at diagnosis.
Sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma (SNUC) or esthesioneuroblastoma may mimic nasal polyps or chronic sinusitis, delaying diagnosis.
Mucosal melanoma of the nasal cavity may be amelanotic and mistaken for inflammatory polyps.
Occult primary with a small HPV-positive oropharyngeal tumor may present only with neck nodes; the primary may be missed without tonsillectomy and lingual tonsillectomy.

red_flags: Persistent hoarseness >3 weeks, especially in smokers or drinkers [GLOT-1].
Unilateral neck mass >2 cm that persists >2 weeks, particularly if firm, fixed, or matted [ORPH-1, OCC-1].
Non-healing oral ulcer >2 weeks, especially with exophytic growth or bleeding [OR-1].
Progressive dysphagia or odynophagia with unintentional weight loss [HYPO-1].
Unilateral otalgia without ear pathology, particularly with concomitant throat symptoms [ORPH-1].
Epistaxis or nasal obstruction that is unilateral, persistent, or associated with cranial neuropathy or facial numbness [NASO-1, ETHM-1].
Facial nerve palsy (VII) in the setting of a parotid mass [SALI-1].
Trismus, proptosis, or periorbital edema suggesting advanced disease [ADV-1].
Cranial nerve deficits (II–VI, IX–XII) without clear alternate cause [NASO-1].
New onset serous otitis media in an adult (>40 years), especially unilateral, should prompt nasopharyngeal evaluation [NASO-1].

diagnostic.rationale: Complete head and neck exam including mirror and fiberoptic laryngoscopy/nasopharyngoscopy to visualize primary site and assess nodal basins.
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification of mucosal lesion, vocal cord mobility, neck node characteristics.

diagnostic.test: Biopsy of primary site or fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of neck node
diagnostic.rationale: Histopathologic confirmation of malignancy; FNA is preferred for neck masses. Image-guided (US or CT) needle biopsy improves yield for cystic nodes.
diagnostic.key_findings: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) most common; additional stains for p16, HPV, EBV as indicated.

diagnostic.test: CT with contrast of primary and neck
diagnostic.rationale: Evaluate tumor extent, bone erosion, cartilage invasion, nodal disease. Thin cuts (<1.25 mm) for laryngeal assessment.
diagnostic.key_findings: Primary tumor size, depth of invasion, cortical bone involvement, nodal size and extranodal extension.

diagnostic.test: MRI with and without contrast of primary and neck
diagnostic.rationale: Preferred for skull base, perineural spread, intracranial extension, bone marrow invasion, and differentiating tumor from obstructed sinuses.
diagnostic.key_findings: Soft tissue delineation, perineural tumor spread, dural involvement.

diagnostic.rationale: Higher sensitivity for nodal and distant metastases; preferred for staging locoregionally advanced disease (T3–4 or N+) and for unknown primary.
diagnostic.key_findings: Metabolic activity of primary, nodal, and distant sites; guides treatment planning.

diagnostic.rationale: Direct visualization, palpation, and biopsy of suspicious areas; essential for occult primary workup.
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification of primary site, synchronous primaries.

diagnostic.rationale: Assess for pulmonary metastases or mediastinal adenopathy; also screens for second primary lung cancer.

diagnostic.test: Panoramic dental x-ray (Panorex) or dental CT
diagnostic.rationale: Pre-treatment dental evaluation for oral cavity cancers requiring mandibulectomy or when postoperative RT is anticipated.
diagnostic.key_findings: Dental health, mandibular height, periapical pathology.

staging.test: CT with contrast of neck and primary
staging.indication: All patients with head and neck cancer to assess T and N stage; extends to carina if low neck nodes or mediastinal involvement suspected [IMG-A].
staging.rationale: Defines tumor size, extension, and nodal characteristics including extranodal extension.

staging.test: MRI with contrast of neck and primary
staging.indication: When perineural spread, skull base invasion, or intracranial extension is suspected; also for dental artifact [IMG-A].
staging.rationale: Superior soft tissue contrast for perineural and bone marrow assessment.

staging.indication: Preferred for locoregionally advanced disease (T3–4, N+) to detect distant metastases; also for N0 neck in select cases, and for post-treatment response assessment at 3–6 months [IMG-A].
staging.rationale: Whole-body staging with high sensitivity for nodal and distant disease.

staging.test: Chest CT with contrast or non-contrast low-dose CT
staging.indication: If FDG-PET/CT not performed; low-dose CT is sufficient for lung parenchymal screening but not for mediastinal nodes [IMG-A].
staging.rationale: Evaluate pulmonary metastases and mediastinal adenopathy.

staging.indication: For mucosal melanoma, high-grade neuroendocrine carcinomas, or when brain metastases or skull base invasion is suspected [IMG-A].
staging.rationale: High sensitivity for brain and meningeal involvement.

staging.indication: For nasopharyngeal carcinoma; prognostic and used for monitoring [NASO-1].
staging.rationale: Elevated pretreatment EBV DNA correlates with stage and survival; post-treatment levels guide surveillance.

biomarkers.rationale: Surrogate marker for HPV-mediated oropharyngeal cancer; required for oropharyngeal primaries.
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis for all oropharyngeal cancers; considered for occult primary with SCC.

biomarkers.rationale: Confirmatory when p16 IHC is discordant or in clinical trials; recommended in low-prevalence regions or for non-oropharyngeal sites.
biomarkers.when: Following p16 IHC if discordant or for trial eligibility; for sinonasal tumors.

biomarkers.test: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) testing (EBER ISH or PCR)
biomarkers.rationale: Detects EBV in tumor tissue; essential for nasopharyngeal carcinoma diagnosis and risk stratification.
biomarkers.when: For nonkeratinizing or undifferentiated nasopharyngeal histology.

biomarkers.rationale: Guides use of pembrolizumab in recurrent/metastatic SCC; required for first-line immunotherapy decisions.
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis for locally advanced or metastatic disease; for all head and neck SCC.

biomarkers.rationale: Identifies actionable biomarkers including HER2, NTRK, FGFR, BRAF, RET, MSI, TMB, dMMR; guides targeted therapy in recurrent/metastatic disease.
biomarkers.when: At progression or for unresectable/metastatic disease; for salivary gland tumors at baseline.

biomarkers.test: Tumor mutational burden (TMB) and MSI/MMR testing
biomarkers.rationale: Identifies patients eligible for pembrolizumab (TMB-H ≥10 mut/Mb, MSI-H/dMMR).
biomarkers.when: For recurrent/metastatic disease after progression on platinum.

biomarkers.rationale: For salivary duct carcinoma; guides hormone therapy.
biomarkers.when: For advanced salivary gland tumors, especially adenoid cystic and salivary duct.

biomarkers.rationale: For salivary gland tumors; guides trastuzumab-based therapy.
biomarkers.when: For advanced salivary gland tumors per ASCO/CAP guidelines.

do_not_miss: p16 IHC for all oropharyngeal cancers and for occult primary SCC [ORPH-B].
PD-L1 CPS testing for all SCC to determine candidacy for pembrolizumab [SYST-A].
Contrast-enhanced CT or MRI of the neck for all patients before treatment [IMG-A].
FDG-PET/CT for locoregionally advanced disease and for unknown primary [IMG-A].
Dental evaluation including Panorex before RT to minimize osteoradionecrosis risk [DENT-A].
avoid: Open biopsy of a neck node without preparation for possible neck dissection; if necessary, prepare patient for definitive surgery [SURG-A].
Canine fossa puncture or Caldwell-Luc approach for maxillary sinus biopsy due to risk of tumor seeding [MAXI-1].
Routine brain MRI for all head and neck cancers; only indicated for mucosal melanoma, high-grade neuroendocrine, or clinical suspicion [IMG-A].
Early PET/CT (<12 weeks) after RT due to high false-positive rate [IMG-A].
Routine annual surveillance imaging in asymptomatic patients with negative 3-month PET/CT; no proven survival benefit [IMG-A].

staging.system: American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) 8th and 9th editions, TNM classification. For most head and neck sites, AJCC 8th edition is used (oral cavity, oropharynx p16-negative, hypopharynx, larynx, nasal cavity/paranasal sinuses, major salivary glands, mucosal melanoma, occult primary). For nasopharynx, AJCC 9th edition (2021) is now adopted. For HPV-mediated oropharyngeal cancer, AJCC 8th edition with separate staging is used, but 9th edition updates are noted. [ST-1 through ST-19]

t_stages.description: Primary tumor cannot be assessed.
Carcinoma in situ.
Tumor ≤2 cm with depth of invasion (DOI) ≤5 mm.
Tumor ≤2 cm with DOI >5 mm, or tumor >2 cm and ≤4 cm with DOI ≤10 mm.
Tumor >2 cm and ≤4 cm with DOI >10 mm, or tumor >4 cm with DOI ≤10 mm.

Moderately advanced local disease: tumor >4 cm with DOI >10 mm or invades adjacent structures (through cortical bone of mandible/maxilla, maxillary sinus, skin of face). Superficial erosion of bone/tooth socket alone by gingival primary is not T4.
Very advanced local disease: tumor invades masticator space, pterygoid plates, skull base, and/or encases internal carotid artery.
Tumor ≤2 cm in greatest dimension.
Tumor >2 cm but ≤4 cm.
Tumor >4 cm or extension to lingual surface of epiglottis.
Moderately advanced: tumor invades larynx, extrinsic muscle of tongue, medial pterygoid, hard palate, or mandible.
Very advanced: tumor invades lateral pterygoid muscle, pterygoid plates, lateral nasopharynx, skull base, or encases carotid artery.
Tumor ≤2 cm in greatest dimension.
Tumor >2 cm but ≤4 cm.
Tumor >4 cm or extension to lingual surface of epiglottis.

Moderately advanced local disease: tumor invades larynx, extrinsic muscle of tongue, medial pterygoid, hard palate, mandible, or beyond. T4b is not used for HPV-mediated disease; all T4 is considered T4.
Tumor limited to one subsite of hypopharynx and/or ≤2 cm.
Tumor invades more than one subsite or adjacent site, or >2 cm but ≤4 cm without fixation of hemilarynx.
Tumor >4 cm or with fixation of hemilarynx or extension to esophageal mucosa.
Moderately advanced: tumor invades thyroid/cricoid cartilage, hyoid bone, thyroid gland, esophageal muscle, or central compartment soft tissue.
Very advanced: tumor invades prevertebral fascia, encases carotid artery, or involves mediastinal structures.
Carcinoma in situ.
Tumor limited to one subsite of supraglottis with normal vocal cord mobility.
Tumor invades mucosa of more than one adjacent subsite of supraglottis or glottis or region outside supraglottis (e.g., base of tongue, vallecula, medial wall of pyriform sinus) without fixation.
Tumor limited to larynx with vocal cord fixation and/or invades postcricoid area, preepiglottic space, paraglottic space, and/or inner cortex of thyroid cartilage.
Moderately advanced: tumor invades through outer cortex of thyroid cartilage and/or invades tissues beyond larynx (trachea, soft tissues of neck, strap muscles, thyroid, esophagus).
Very advanced: tumor invades prevertebral space, encases carotid artery, or invades mediastinal structures.
Tumor limited to vocal cord(s) (may involve anterior or posterior commissure) with normal mobility. T1a: one cord; T1b: both cords.
Tumor extends to supraglottis and/or subglottis, and/or with impaired vocal cord mobility.
Tumor limited to larynx with vocal cord fixation and/or invasion of paraglottic space and/or inner cortex of thyroid cartilage.

Moderately advanced: tumor invades through outer cortex of thyroid cartilage and/or invades tissues beyond larynx (trachea, cricoid cartilage, soft tissues of neck, strap muscles, thyroid, esophagus).
Very advanced: tumor invades prevertebral space, encases carotid artery, or invades mediastinal structures.
Tumor limited to subglottis.
Tumor extends to vocal cord(s) with normal or impaired mobility.
Tumor limited to larynx with vocal cord fixation and/or inner cortex of thyroid cartilage.
Moderately advanced: tumor invades cricoid or thyroid cartilage and/or invades tissues beyond larynx (trachea, soft tissues of neck, strap muscles, thyroid, esophagus).
Very advanced: tumor invades prevertebral space, encases carotid artery, or invades mediastinal structures.
Tumor confined to nasopharynx, or extension to oropharynx and/or nasal cavity without parapharyngeal involvement.
Tumor with extension to parapharyngeal space, and/or adjacent soft tissue involvement (medial pterygoid, lateral pterygoid, prevertebral muscles).
Tumor with unequivocal infiltration of bony structures at skull base, cervical vertebra, pterygoid structures, and/or paranasal sinuses.

Tumor with intracranial extension, involvement of cranial nerves, hypopharynx, orbit, parotid gland, and/or extensive soft tissue infiltration beyond the anterolateral surface of the lateral pterygoid muscle.
Tumor limited to maxillary sinus mucosa with no erosion or destruction of bone.
Tumor causing bone erosion or destruction including extension into hard palate and/or middle nasal meatus, except extension to posterior wall of maxillary sinus and pterygoid plates.
Tumor invades bone of posterior wall of maxillary sinus, subcutaneous tissues, floor or medial wall of orbit, pterygoid fossa, ethmoid sinuses.
Moderately advanced: tumor invades anterior orbital contents, skin of cheek, pterygoid plates, infratemporal fossa, cribriform plate, sphenoid or frontal sinuses.
Very advanced: tumor invades orbital apex, dura, brain, middle cranial fossa, cranial nerves other than V2, nasopharynx, or clivus.
Tumor restricted to any one subsite, with or without bony invasion.
Tumor invading two subsites within a single region or extending to involve an adjacent region within the nasoethmoidal complex, with or without bony invasion.
Tumor extends to invade medial wall or floor of orbit, maxillary sinus, palate, or cribriform plate.
Moderately advanced: tumor invades anterior orbital contents, skin of nose or cheek, minimal extension to anterior cranial fossa, pterygoid plates, sphenoid or frontal sinuses.
Very advanced: tumor invades orbital apex, dura, brain, middle cranial fossa, cranial nerves other than V2, nasopharynx, or clivus.
Tumor ≤2 cm in greatest dimension without extraparenchymal extension.
Tumor >2 cm but ≤4 cm without extraparenchymal extension.
Tumor >4 cm and/or having extraparenchymal extension.
Moderately advanced: tumor invades skin, mandible, ear canal, and/or facial nerve.
Very advanced: tumor invades skull base and/or pterygoid plates and/or encases carotid artery.

t_stages.stage: T3 – Mucosal Melanoma of Head and Neck
t_stages.description: Tumors limited to the mucosa and immediately underlying soft tissue, regardless of thickness or greatest dimension (e.g., polypoid nasal disease, pigmented or nonpigmented lesions of oral cavity, pharynx, larynx).
Moderately advanced: tumor involving deep soft tissue, cartilage, bone, or overlying skin.
Very advanced: tumor involving brain, dura, skull base, lower cranial nerves (IX, X, XI, XII), masticator space, carotid artery, prevertebral space, or mediastinal structures.
No primary tumor identified after appropriate workup; used for staging cervical lymph node metastases from unknown primary.

Metastases in multiple ipsilateral nodes, none >6 cm, ENE(-).

Distant metastasis with more than 3 areas of spread.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Small tumor with DOI ≤5 mm, no nodal metastases.

Curative; chemoradiation (category 1 for ENE).

staging_pearls: HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer has a separate staging system (AJCC 8th ed.) with better prognosis; T4b is not used and N staging is simplified based on size and laterality [ST-7].
For oral cavity, depth of invasion (DOI) is integrated into T staging; a tumor >4 cm with DOI >10 mm is T4a [ST-1].
Extranodal extension (ENE) is a critical risk factor and is incorporated into both clinical and pathological N staging for most sites; clinically overt ENE is designated cN3b [ST-1, ST-5].
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma staging uses AJCC 9th edition with distinct N and M categories including retropharyngeal nodes and advanced radiologic ENE [ST-3].
Mucosal melanoma staging begins at T3 (limited to mucosa) and T4a/b, reflecting its aggressive nature; there are no T1–2 categories [ST-19].
Occult primary (cervical node metastasis without known primary) is staged as T0, N1–3, M0–1 using the cervical lymph node and unknown primary table [ST-15].
Salivary gland tumors include extraparenchymal extension in T3 and above [ST-17].
Pathological staging for HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer uses node count (≤4 vs. >4) rather than size and ENE [ST-7].
For laryngeal cancer, T3 includes vocal cord fixation and/or paraglottic space invasion; T4a requires through-cartilage invasion [ST-8].
Sinonasal tumors have separate T staging for maxillary sinus and ethmoid sinus/nasal cavity [ST-11].
Stage grouping for most sites uses AJCC 8th ed. prognostic stage groups; survival data are derived from older cohorts and may overestimate modern outcomes with improved treatment.
cN0 neck assessment is challenging; FDG-PET/CT has a negative predictive value of 93% for nodal metastases [Discussion MS-8].

management_principles.overview: The management of head and neck cancers requires a multidisciplinary approach integrating surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, and a wide range of support services. The NCCN guidelines emphasize that all patients need access to the full range of specialists with expertise in the comprehensive care of head and neck cancer for optimal treatment and follow-up [TEAM-1]. Outcomes are improved when patients are treated at high-volume centers [Discussion]. The choice of treatment modality—single-modality (surgery or RT) versus combined modality (e.g., surgery plus radiation or chemoradiation)—depends on the specific site of disease, histology, stage, and baseline comorbid conditions [Discussion]. Single-modality treatment is generally recommended for early-stage (stage I or II) HPV-unrelated cancers. Surgery is usually preferred for oral cavity and paranasal sinus cancers, while RT with or without chemotherapy is nearly always preferred for all stages of nasopharyngeal carcinoma and more advanced stages of HPV-related oropharyngeal cancer [Discussion]. For the approximately 60% of patients with locally or regionally advanced disease at diagnosis, combined modality therapy is generally recommended [Discussion]. Equipoise exists between the choices of surgery or RT as the primary treatment modality for stage I and II HPV-associated and -unassociated disease, often based on local institutional expertise and/or perceived relative morbidity [Discussion]. Participation in clinical trials is a preferred or recommended treatment option in many situations [Discussion]. The NCCN Panel has tried to create clinical trials with evidence-based practices while providing a statement of consensus as to the acceptable range of treatment options [Discussion]. Treatment should be individualized based on patient characteristics and goals of therapy [SYST-A 1 of 7]. Key components for all patients include smoking cessation counseling and alcohol use counseling, as well as screening for distress [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1]. Fertility/reproductive counseling should be offered [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1]. Screening for hepatitis B for patients undergoing cancer therapy is recommended [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1].

by_intent.intent: Curative-intent treatment for newly diagnosed locoregionally confined disease
by_intent.patient_group: Patients with stage I-II (early-stage) disease who are candidates for single-modality therapy; patients with stage III-IV (locoregionally advanced) disease who are candidates for combined modality therapy
by_intent.approach: Early-stage: single-modality treatment with surgery or radiation therapy. Locoregionally advanced: combined modality therapy, typically surgery followed by risk-based adjuvant therapy (RT or systemic therapy/RT), or definitive concurrent systemic therapy/RT, or induction chemotherapy followed by RT or systemic therapy/RT. The specific approach depends on the primary site, HPV status (for oropharynx), and tumor extent.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with laryngeal or hypopharyngeal cancer for whom total laryngectomy is indicated; patients with locally advanced oropharyngeal cancer where surgical morbidity is high
by_intent.approach: Concurrent systemic therapy/RT is the standard approach for larynx preservation in patients with advanced laryngeal cancer, based on the results of the RTOG 91-11 trial [Discussion]. For hypopharynx cancer, induction chemotherapy followed by definitive RT in responders is an alternative to laryngopharyngectomy [Discussion]. In oropharyngeal cancer, definitive RT or systemic therapy/RT is often preferred over upfront surgery for HPV-associated cT4 or cN3 disease to avoid triple modality therapy and additional treatment-induced morbidity [Discussion].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with metastatic (M1) disease at initial presentation, or with recurrent/persistent disease that is unresectable and not amenable to curative-intent reirradiation
by_intent.approach: Enrollment in a clinical trial is preferred. Systemic therapy, with the goal of palliation and survival prolongation, is the mainstay of treatment. Palliative RT may be used for symptom control (e.g., pain, bleeding, airway obstruction). Best supportive care is appropriate for patients with poor performance status [ADV-2, ADV-4]. For patients with newly diagnosed metastatic NPC, systemic therapy with or without consolidative locoregional RT may be considered for selected patients [NASO-3].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: The comprehensive care of patients with head and neck cancers is complex and requires a multidisciplinary team. This team should include expertise from head and neck surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, diagnostic and interventional radiology, plastic and reconstructive surgery, specialized nursing care, dentistry/prosthodontics, physical medicine and rehabilitation (including therapy for lymphedema of the neck), speech and swallowing therapy, clinical social work, clinical nutrition, and pathology (including cytopathology) [TEAM-1]. Adjunctive services such as neurosurgery, ophthalmology, psychiatry, addiction services, audiology, palliative care, and pain management are also essential [TEAM-1]. Follow-up should be performed by a physician and other health care professionals with expertise in the comprehensive care and prevention of treatment sequelae and should include a comprehensive head and neck exam [TEAM-1]. It is critical that multidisciplinary evaluation and treatment be coordinated and integrated prospectively by all disciplines involved in patient care before the initiation of any treatment [SURG-A 1 of 9]. The initial evaluation and treatment planning requires a multidisciplinary team of health care providers with expertise in caring for such patients [Discussion].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) is a critical determinant of treatment selection, particularly in the setting of very advanced (unresectable or metastatic) or recurrent/persistent disease. For newly diagnosed locoregionally advanced unresectable disease (T4b,N0-3 or unresectable primary or nodal disease or unfit for surgery), patients with PS 0-1 are candidates for aggressive concurrent systemic therapy/RT or induction systemic therapy followed by RT or systemic therapy/RT [ADV-1]. Patients with PS 2 may be treated with concurrent systemic therapy/RT (preferred) or RT alone. Patients with PS 3-4 should receive palliative RT, single-agent systemic therapy (for PS 3 only), or best supportive care [ADV-1]. For metastatic (M1) disease at initial presentation, patients with PS 0-1 can receive combination or single-agent systemic therapy, or locoregional treatment for selected patients with limited metastases. Patients with PS 2-3 should receive combination (PS 2 only) or single-agent systemic therapy, or best supportive care. Patients with PS 4 should receive best supportive care ± palliative RT [ADV-2]. The decision to treat with reirradiation should take into account surgical resectability, comorbidities, toxicity of previous treatment, organ dysfunction, and time since previous treatment; before curative intent reirradiation, the patient should have a reasonable ECOG PS of 0-1 [RAD-A 4 of 7]. For patients receiving or who have received checkpoint inhibitor therapies, ongoing monitoring for adverse reactions is required [FOLL-A 1 of 2].

management_pathways.title: Oral Cavity Cancer: T1-2,N0

nodes.criteria: T1-2, N0 oral cavity cancer, patient is a surgical candidate

options.label: Resection of primary ± neck dissection (guided by tumor location, depth of invasion, and imaging)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant treatment is determined by pathologic findings from neck dissection. For SLN pN0, no further neck surgery is needed. If SLN pN+ or SLN identification unsuccessful, a neck dissection is performed. Pathologic assessment of the resected primary and neck determines the need for adjuvant RT or systemic therapy/RT based on the presence of adverse pathologic features (extranodal extension, positive margins, close margins, pT3/pT4, pN2/pN3, nodal disease in levels IV/V, perineural, vascular, or lymphatic invasion). For no positive nodes and no adverse features: observation. For one positive node without adverse features: consider RT. For adverse features without positive margin: consider RT (for other risk features) or systemic therapy/RT (for extranodal extension ± positive margin). For positive margin: re-resection if feasible and consider RT if negative margins, or consider systemic therapy/RT [OR-2].

nodes.criteria: T1-2, N0 oral cavity cancer, patient is not a surgical candidate or declines surgery

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy is applicable after definitive RT. Follow-up includes post-treatment neck evaluation [FOLL-A 2 of 2].

management_pathways.title: Oral Cavity Cancer: T3,N0 or T1-3,N1-3 or T4a,N0-3 (Resectable)

options.label: Resection of primary ± ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection
Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab (if PD-L1 positive [CPS ≥ 1], except for N3 disease)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant treatment is based on pathologic findings from surgery. For N0,N1,N2a-b,N3: resection of primary ± ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection. For N2c (bilateral): resection of primary and bilateral neck dissection. No adverse pathologic features: consider RT. Adverse pathologic features: if extranodal extension ± positive margin → systemic therapy/RT (category 1); if other risk features → RT or consider systemic therapy/RT. Positive margin: systemic therapy/RT (category 1) or re-resection if feasible and consider RT if negative margins. If neoadjuvant pembrolizumab was received: RT + pembrolizumab (with cisplatin if extranodal extension and/or positive margin) followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab [OR-3].

options.label: Treatment for Very Advanced Head and Neck Cancer (ADV-1)

adjuvant_logic.summary: These patients are managed under the very advanced cancer pathway.

management_pathways.title: Oropharyngeal Cancer (p16-negative): T1-2,N0-1

nodes.criteria: T1-2, N0-1, p16-negative, patient is a candidate for either primary modality

options.label: Resection of primary and ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection
Concurrent systemic therapy/RT (for T1-2,N1 only, category 2B)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant treatment after surgery is guided by pathologic findings. For pN0 and no adverse pathologic features: observation. For pN1 without other adverse features: consider RT. For adverse features with extranodal extension ± positive margin: systemic therapy/RT. For other risk features: RT or consider systemic therapy/RT. For positive margin: re-resection if feasible, or RT, or systemic therapy/RT. If neoadjuvant pembrolizumab was received: RT + pembrolizumab (with cisplatin if extranodal extension and/or positive margin) followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab [ORPH-2].

management_pathways.title: Oropharyngeal Cancer (p16-negative): T3-4a,N0-1 or T1-4a,N2-3

options.label: Induction chemotherapy (category 3) followed by RT or systemic therapy/RT
Resection of primary and ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients, adjuvant treatment is based on pathologic findings. No adverse features: RT. Adverse features with extranodal extension and/or positive margin: systemic therapy/RT. Other risk features: RT or systemic therapy/RT. If neoadjuvant pembrolizumab received: RT + pembrolizumab (with cisplatin if extranodal extension and/or positive margin) followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab [ORPH-3].

options.label: Induction chemotherapy (category 3) followed by RT or systemic therapy/RT
Resection of primary and ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients: No adverse features: RT. Adverse features with extranodal extension and/or positive margin: systemic therapy/RT. Other risk features: RT or systemic therapy/RT. If neoadjuvant pembrolizumab received (except for N3 disease): RT + pembrolizumab (with cisplatin if extranodal extension and/or positive margin) followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab [ORPH-4].

management_pathways.title: Oropharyngeal Cancer (p16 [HPV]-positive): T1-2,N0 or T0-2,N1 (single node ≤3 cm)

options.label: Resection of primary and ipsilateral or bilateral selective neck dissection

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant treatment based on pathologic features. No adverse features: observation. Adverse features with extranodal extension ± positive margin: systemic therapy/RT or RT (category 2B). Other risk features: RT or systemic therapy/RT (category 2B). Positive margin: re-resection if feasible or systemic therapy/RT or RT [ORPHPV-1].

options.label: Resection of primary and ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant treatment based on pathologic features. No adverse features: observation. Adverse features with extranodal extension ± positive margin: systemic therapy/RT or RT (category 2B). Positive margin: re-resection if feasible or systemic therapy/RT or RT (category 2B). Other risk features: RT (de-escalation to 50 Gy may be considered [category 2B]) or systemic therapy/RT (category 2B) [ORPHPV-2].

management_pathways.title: Oropharyngeal Cancer (p16 [HPV]-positive): T0-2,N1 (>3cm or 2+ ipsilateral ≤6cm), N2, T3,N0-2 or T0-3,N3, T4,N0-3

nodes.criteria: T0-2,N1 (single node >3 cm, or 2 or more ipsilateral nodes ≤6 cm), or T0-2,N2, or T3,N0-2

options.label: Resection of primary and ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection (not recommended if clinical evidence of fixed/matted nodes or obvious extranodal extension)

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients: No adverse features: observation. Adverse features with extranodal extension and/or positive margin: systemic therapy/RT. Other risk features: RT (de-escalation to 50 Gy may be considered [category 2B]) or consider systemic therapy/RT [ORPHPV-3].

options.label: Resection of primary and ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection (not recommended if clinical evidence of fixed/matted nodes or obvious extranodal extension)

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients: No adverse features: observation. Adverse features with extranodal extension and/or positive margin: systemic therapy/RT. Other risk features: RT or consider systemic therapy/RT [ORPHPV-4].

management_pathways.title: Nasopharyngeal Cancer: T1,N0,M0 or T2,N0,M0 or T0(EBV+)-2,N1,M0 or T3,N0,M0

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant systemic therapy is routinely recommended for T1,N0,M0. For T2,N0,M0, concurrent systemic therapy may be indicated in the presence of high-risk features such as bulky tumor volume or high serum EBV DNA copy number [NASO-2].

options.label: Definitive RT ± concurrent systemic therapy if high-risk features (bulky tumor volume or high serum EBV DNA copy number)

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant systemic therapy is routinely recommended, but it may be considered for patients with high-risk features [NASO-2].

management_pathways.title: Nasopharyngeal Cancer: T3,N1-3,M0 or T4,N0-3,M0 or T0(EBV+)-2,N2-3,M0

options.label: Induction chemotherapy followed by systemic therapy/RT (preferred, category 1)
Concurrent systemic therapy/RT followed by adjuvant chemotherapy

adjuvant_logic.summary: For patients treated with induction chemotherapy followed by systemic therapy/RT, no additional adjuvant therapy is typically planned. For patients treated with concurrent systemic therapy/RT alone (category 2B), no adjuvant therapy is given. For patients treated with concurrent systemic therapy/RT followed by adjuvant chemotherapy, the preferred regimen is cisplatin/infusional fluorouracil. Other options include carboplatin/infusional fluorouracil. Useful in certain circumstances: capecitabine for EBV-associated disease [NASO-2, NASO-B 1 of 4].

management_pathways.title: Nasopharyngeal Cancer: M1 at initial presentation
branching_basis: Extent of metastatic disease (oligometastatic vs widely metastatic)

options.label: Induction chemotherapy, then RT to primary and regional nodes and to oligometastases as indicated, or cisplatin/RT, or maintenance capecitabine

adjuvant_logic.summary: After induction chemotherapy, if CR or near CR is achieved, consider definitive RT to primary and regional nodes (preferred) and to oligometastases as indicated, or continued systemic therapy [NASO-3].
No adjuvant RT is typically indicated unless for palliation.

management_pathways.title: Hypopharyngeal Cancer: Amenable to larynx-preserving surgery (most T1,N0 and selected T2,N0)

nodes.criteria: Most T1,N0, selected T2,N0, amenable to larynx-preserving (conservation) surgery

options.label: Partial laryngopharyngectomy (open or endoscopic) + ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection ± hemithyroidectomy, and pretracheal and ipsilateral paratracheal lymph node dissection

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients: pN0 and no adverse features: observation. pN1 without other adverse features: consider RT. Adverse features with extranodal extension ± positive margin: systemic therapy/RT (category 1). Other risk features: RT or consider systemic therapy/RT (for T2 only). Positive margin: re-resection if feasible, or RT, or consider systemic therapy/RT (for T2 only) [HYPO-2].

management_pathways.title: Hypopharyngeal Cancer: Advanced requiring pharyngectomy (T2-3,N0-3, T1,N+) or T4a,N0-3

nodes.criteria: T2-3,N0-3 (requiring pharyngectomy with partial or total laryngectomy), T1,N+

options.label: Partial or total laryngopharyngectomy + ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection ± thyroidectomy and pretracheal/paratracheal lymph node dissection

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients: pN0 and no adverse features: observation. pN1 without other adverse features: consider RT. Adverse features with extranodal extension and/or positive margin: systemic therapy/RT (category 1). Other risk features: RT or consider systemic therapy/RT. If neoadjuvant pembrolizumab received: RT + pembrolizumab (with cisplatin if extranodal extension and/or positive margin) followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab [HYPO-3]. For patients treated with induction chemotherapy, response assessment determines subsequent treatment [HYPO-4].

options.label: Total laryngopharyngectomy + ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection ± thyroidectomy, after ipsilateral or bilateral paratracheal lymph node dissection

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients: Extranodal extension and/or positive margin: systemic therapy/RT (category 1). Other risk features: RT or consider systemic therapy/RT. If neoadjuvant pembrolizumab received: RT + pembrolizumab (with cisplatin if extranodal extension and/or positive margin) followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab [HYPO-5].

management_pathways.title: Glottic Larynx Cancer: Carcinoma in situ, T1-2,N0, select T3,N0

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy is typically needed for carcinoma in situ treated with complete endoscopic resection.

nodes.criteria: T1-2,N0 or select T3,N0, amenable to larynx-preserving (conservation) surgery

options.label: Partial laryngectomy/endoscopic or open resection as indicated and neck dissection as indicated

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients: No adverse features: observation. pN1 without other risk features: consider RT. Adverse features with extranodal extension: systemic therapy/RT (category 1). Positive margin: re-resection if feasible, or RT. Other risk features: RT [GLOT-2].

management_pathways.title: Glottic Larynx Cancer: T3,N0-3 or T4a,N0-3

options.label: Surgery, including ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection; consider thyroidectomy to clear central compartment nodes

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients: pN0 and no adverse features: observation. pN1 without other risk features: consider RT. Adverse features with extranodal extension and/or positive margin: systemic therapy/RT (category 1). Other risk features: RT or consider systemic therapy/RT. If neoadjuvant pembrolizumab received: RT + pembrolizumab (with cisplatin if extranodal extension and/or positive margin) followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab [GLOT-3].

options.label: Surgery, including ipsilateral or bilateral neck dissection; thyroidectomy to clear central compartment nodes, especially when there is extralaryngeal extension of the thyroid gland and significant subglottic extension
Consider concurrent systemic therapy/RT or clinical trial for function-preserving management
Induction chemotherapy (for selected patients who decline surgery)

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgical patients: pN0 and no adverse features: consider RT. pN1 without other risk features: consider RT. Adverse features with extranodal extension and/or positive margin: systemic therapy/RT (category 1). Other risk features: RT or consider systemic therapy/RT. If neoadjuvant pembrolizumab received: RT + pembrolizumab (with cisplatin if extranodal extension and/or positive margin) followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab [GLOT-6].

nodes.criteria: Selected patients with T4a primary who decline surgery

options.label: Clinical trial for function-preserving surgical or RT management

management_pathways.title: Very Advanced Head and Neck Cancer: Newly Diagnosed (M0) T4b,N0-3 or Unresectable/Unfit for Surgery

options.label: Concurrent systemic therapy/RT (preferred systemic agent: cisplatin, category 1)
Induction systemic therapy followed by RT or systemic therapy/RT
Concurrent systemic therapy/RT (preferred, with docetaxel as an option for cisplatin-ineligible patients)

management_pathways.title: Very Advanced Head and Neck Cancer: Metastatic (M1) at Initial Presentation

options.label: Combination systemic therapy or single-agent systemic therapy
Surgery or RT or systemic therapy/RT for selected patients with limited metastases
Combination (PS 2 only) or single-agent systemic therapy

management_pathways.title: Recurrent or Persistent Very Advanced Head and Neck Cancer: Locoregional Recurrence

nodes.criteria: Locoregional recurrence or persistent disease without prior RT, resectable

options.label: Surgery ± postoperative reirradiation or systemic therapy/RT, clinical trial preferred
Combination systemic therapy (category 2B) followed by RT or systemic therapy/RT

adjuvant_logic.summary: For completely resected disease: observation. For adverse features with extranodal extension and/or positive margin: systemic therapy/RT (category 1). For other risk features: RT or consider systemic therapy/RT [ADV-3].

nodes.criteria: Locoregional recurrence or persistent disease without prior RT, unresectable

options.label: Combination systemic therapy (category 2B) followed by RT or systemic therapy/RT

nodes.criteria: Locoregional recurrence, second primary, or persistent disease with prior RT, resectable

options.label: Surgery ± postoperative reirradiation, clinical trial preferred
Reirradiation ± systemic therapy, clinical trial preferred

nodes.criteria: Locoregional recurrence, second primary, or persistent disease with prior RT, unresectable

options.label: Reirradiation ± systemic therapy, clinical trial preferred

management_pathways.title: Recurrent or Persistent Disease with Distant Metastases
branching_basis: Extent of disease (distant metastases only vs locoregional + distant)

nodes.criteria: PS 0-1, distant metastases only or locoregional + distant

options.label: Combination systemic therapy or single-agent systemic therapy, clinical trial preferred
Surgery or RT or concurrent systemic therapy/RT for selected patients with limited metastases

nodes.criteria: PS 4, distant metastases only or locoregional + distant

nodes.criteria: Poorly differentiated or nonkeratinizing squamous cell, NOS, or anaplastic (not thyroid) of neck node, or squamous cell carcinoma; node levels I-III

adjuvant_logic.summary: Post neck dissection treatment depends on nodal stage and HPV/EBV status. For N1 without extranodal extension: RT (target volume determined by tumor size, nodal station, and HPV and EBV status) or observe. For N2,N3 without extranodal extension: RT or consider systemic therapy/RT (category 2B). For extranodal extension: systemic therapy/RT (category 1) or RT [OCC-4].

nodes.criteria: Poorly differentiated or nonkeratinizing squamous cell, NOS, or anaplastic (not thyroid) of neck node, or squamous cell carcinoma; N1 disease

management_pathways.title: Salivary Gland Tumors: Clinically Benign or T1,T2 Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: Clinically benign or T1,T2 carcinoma, complete resection possible

options.label: Complete resection (no enucleation of lateral lobe for parotid)

adjuvant_logic.summary: For benign or low-grade tumors: no further treatment. If tumor spillage or perineural invasion, consider RT. For adenoid cystic or intermediate/high-grade tumors: consider RT [SALI-2].

nodes.criteria: Clinically benign or T1,T2, no resection possible or not recommended (T4b)

options.label: Definitive RT or concurrent systemic therapy/RT (category 2B)

nodes.criteria: Completely resected, no adverse pathologic features

options.label: Observation or RT for adenoid cystic

management_pathways.title: Mucosal Melanoma of the Head and Neck: Sinus or Nasal Cavity

options.label: Resection of primary or clinical trial

adjuvant_logic.summary: Strongly consider postoperative RT to primary site ± systemic therapy (category 2B) [MM-2].

nodes.criteria: Sinus or nasal cavity, T3-T4a,N1 or T4a,N0

options.label: Resection + neck dissection of positive neck, or resection alone for T4a,N0

adjuvant_logic.summary: Postoperative RT to primary site and neck ± systemic therapy (category 2B) [MM-2].

nodes.criteria: Sinus or nasal cavity, T4b,N0 or T4b,N1

options.label: Systemic therapy (per NCCN Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous)

items.text: Complete head and neck exam, including mirror and fiberoptic examination as clinically indicated
items.recommendation: Required for all patients at initial workup for all head and neck cancer subsites [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1]

items.text: Documentation and quantification (pack years smoked) of tobacco use history, as well as alcohol use and counseling
items.recommendation: Required for all patients. All patients who currently smoke should be advised to quit smoking, and those who formerly smoked should be advised to remain abstinent from smoking [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1]

items.text: Screen for distress using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health
items.recommendation: Required for all patients [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, NASO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, ETHM-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1]

items.text: Biopsy of primary site or fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of the neck
items.recommendation: Required for all patients. Image-guided (US or CT) needle biopsy of cystic neck nodes may offer better diagnostic yield than FNA by palpation alone. For unresectable or metastatic disease where there is a plan for systemic therapy, a core biopsy would allow for biomarker testing [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1]

items.text: PD-L1 testing by immunohistochemistry (IHC) (combined positive score [CPS])
items.recommendation: Recommended for oral cavity, oropharynx, hypopharynx, larynx, and supraglottic larynx cancers to guide treatment decisions, particularly for neoadjuvant pembrolizumab eligibility and first-line systemic therapy in recurrent/metastatic disease [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1]

items.text: Tumor HPV testing by p16 IHC (for oropharynx cancers)
items.recommendation: Required for all oropharyngeal cancers to determine HPV status. A 70% cutoff with nuclear and cytoplasmic expression with at least moderate to strong intensity is recommended. Confirmatory HPV direct testing (e.g., PCR, RNA ISH) is recommended, especially for clinical trials [ORPH-1, ORPH-B]

Consider for nonkeratinizing or undifferentiated histology. Common detection methods include ISH for EBER or IHC staining for LMP1. EBV DNA load in serum or plasma may be quantified using PCR [NASO-1]

items.text: HPV and EBV testing (for occult primary)
items.recommendation: Suggested for squamous cell or undifferentiated histology to help identify the primary site [OCC-1]

items.text: Multigene panel testing (MGPT) and biomarker testing
items.recommendation: Consider for recurrent or metastatic disease to identify actionable alterations. For nasopharyngeal cancers, test for at least PD-L1 expression, TMB, and MMR prior to treatment (category 2B). For salivary gland tumors, test for AR, HER2, NTRK1/2/3, FGFR, BRAF, RET, MSI, dMMR, TMB, and PD-L1 (category 2B). For non-nasopharyngeal cancers, testing for at least PD-L1 expression, MSI, MMR, TMB, HER2, and FGFR may be considered to guide treatment options [NASO-B, SALI-B, SYST-A 1 of 7]
Consider in the context of early age of onset or family history [OR-1]

items.text: CT with contrast and/or MRI with and without contrast of primary and neck
items.recommendation: Essential for staging and treatment planning for most head and neck cancers. MRI is preferred for skull base invasion, cranial nerve involvement, and perineural tumor spread. CT is complementary for evaluating cortical bone erosion [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, IMG-A 1 of 4]

Recommended as clinically indicated for most sites to evaluate for pulmonary metastases or synchronous primary. For locoregionally advanced cancer, FDG-PET/CT is preferred to evaluate for distant disease [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, IMG-A 2 of 4]

Consider for many sites, particularly for detecting locoregional nodal and distant metastases, for staging before definitive treatment, and for post-treatment response assessment. Preferred over PET scan alone [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, IMG-A]

Recommended for many sites. For oropharynx cancer, prior to treatment, EUA with biopsy confirmation of the oropharyngeal primary site is recommended for patients presenting with a p16+ cervical lymph node [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, OCC-2]

items.text: Panoramic dental x-ray (Panorex) or dental CT without contrast
items.recommendation: Recommended for oral cavity cancers requiring mandibulotomy and/or mandibulectomy, and as part of comprehensive pre-radiation dental evaluation [OR-1, IMG-A 1 of 4, DENT-A]

items.text: Imaging for distant metastases (FDG-PET/CT and/or chest CT with contrast; bone scan if PET/CT not done)
items.recommendation: Recommended for nasopharyngeal cancer [NASO-1]

items.text: MRI with and without contrast of skull base to clavicle
items.recommendation: Recommended for nasopharyngeal cancer to evaluate skull base erosion [NASO-1]

Recommended for most patients, particularly those expected to receive RT. Goals include patient education, elimination of potential sources of infection, and prescription of preventive protocols [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1, DENT-A]

Recommended for most patients at baseline, especially for those with speech/swallowing dysfunction or whose treatment is likely to affect speech/swallowing [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1, NUTR-A]
Recommended as clinically indicated for oropharynx, hypopharynx, larynx, supraglottic larynx, and nasopharynx cancers [ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1]
Consider for conservation surgery candidates for hypopharynx, glottic larynx, and supraglottic larynx cancers [HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1]
Recommended as clinically indicated for all surgical candidates [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, SALI-1]
Offered to patients with these concerns [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1]

Recommended for all patients anticipating systemic therapy. Patients should have documentation of vaccination or be screened through three tests (HBsAg, HBcAb total Ig or IgG, and antibody to HBsAg) prior to or at the beginning of systemic anticancer therapy [OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1]
Consider for patients with nasopharyngeal cancer [NASO-1]

Recommended as clinically indicated for all patients. The comprehensive care of patients with head and neck cancers is complex and all patients need access to the full range of support services and specialists [TEAM-1, OR-1, ORPH-1, HYPO-1, GLOT-1, SUPRA-1, NASO-1, ETHM-1, MAXI-1, OCC-1, SALI-1, MM-1]

surgery.role: Surgery is a cornerstone of treatment for many head and neck cancers, particularly for resectable disease. For oral cavity and paranasal sinus cancers, surgery is usually preferred. For early-stage oropharyngeal, laryngeal, and hypopharyngeal cancers, surgery is an effective alternative to RT. For advanced disease, surgery is often combined with postoperative RT or systemic therapy/RT. The goal of surgery is complete tumor resection with histologic verification of tumor-free margins, maximizing survival while preserving form and function [Discussion, SURG-A]. Resection of the primary tumor should be planned based on the extent of the primary tumor as ascertained by clinical examination and careful interpretation of appropriate radiographic images. En bloc resection of the primary tumor should be attempted whenever feasible [SURG-A 3 of 9]. For patients undergoing an operation, the surgical procedure, margins, and reconstructive plan should be developed and designed to resect all gross tumors with adequate tumor-free surgical margins. The surgical procedure should rarely be modified based on any response observed as a result of prior therapy except in instances of tumor progression that mandate a more extensive procedure [SURG-A 1 of 9].
